summary.brief: Easterling v. Kendall, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: On September 5, 2011, Alesa Easterling, then fifteen years old, was swimming at the YMCA in Twin Falls, Idaho, when she fell from a large floating structure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: After falling, Easterling experienced a severe headache, vomiting, and numbness in her left arm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: When paramedics arrived, they noted that Easterling was alert but was experiencing an obvious left facial droop and slurred speech.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Upon arriving at St. Luke’s, Easterling was examined by emergency room physician Dr. Kendall.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Kendall ordered a CT scan and basic testing of Easterling, which did not reveal any abnormalities.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Believing that Easterling was suffering from a concussion, Kendall consulted the on-call neurologist, who agreed with his diagnosis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The next morning, Easterling was taken to the emergency room in Twin Falls because she was experiencing a severe headache and nausea and her father had observed her twitching in her sleep.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A CT Scan and MRI were performed. After reviewing the MRI, a radiologist concluded that Easterling had a dissection of the right internal carotid artery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Easterling was again air lifted to St. Luke’s in Boise. Treating physicians at St. Luke’s in Boise agreed that Easterling had suffered a stroke due to a carotid artery dissection.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Easterling remained in intensive care from September 6 to September 8, 2011.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 8, Easterling experienced another stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Easterling alleges that she has suffered permanent neurological damage due to the series of strokes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Wapen would testify that Kendall should have diagnosed Easterling with a carotid artery dissection and started Easterling on anticoagulant treatment on September 5th and the failure to do so was a substantial factor in causing her further stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The district court granted Kendall’s motion for a directed verdict, holding that Easterling failed to provide substantial evidence to prove proximate cause.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The district court reasoned that a jury is simply not qualified to determine whether Dr. Kendall’s failure to properly diagnose and the potential delay in treatment were a substantial cause of Easterling’s injuries without reliance on expert testimony or evidence.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: We affirm the district court’s decisions to grant a directed verdict to Kendall, to exclude Easterling’s expert opinion testimony on causation, and to deny Easterling’s motion to present rebuttal evidence in her case in chief.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe headache, vomiting, and numbness in left arm after falling
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient fell from a large floating structure while swimming, experienced severe headache, vomiting, and numbness in left arm.

focused_exam.neurological: Subtle left side facial asymmetry, left facial droop, slurred speech

reveal.clinical_significance: Did not reveal cause of symptoms
reveal.decision_impact: Did not lead to further imaging or diagnosis

requestables.name: MRI
requestables.description: MRI of the head and neck

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms after fall

key_events.event: Patient airlifted to St. Luke’s Regional Medical Center

timeline_phases.name: Subsequent Hospitalization
timeline_phases.description: Patient re-evaluated and diagnosed with carotid artery dissection

key_events.event: MRI performed, carotid artery dissection diagnosed

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of stroke
decision_points.prompt: What imaging investigation should be ordered?

options.description: Order MRI of the head and neck
options.clinical_reasoning: MRI is more sensitive for detecting carotid artery dissection
CT scan is less sensitive for detecting carotid artery dissection

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established MRI is standard for suspected carotid artery dissection

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to order MRI led to misdiagnosis
explanation.what_should_have_happened: MRI would have identified carotid artery dissection
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered permanent neurological damage due to strokes

key_findings: Directed verdict for defendant due to lack of causation evidence
Court found no breach of standard of care

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: MRI would have shown carotid artery dissection

factual_timeline.event: Patient fell and experienced severe headache, vomiting, and numbness
MRI performed, carotid artery dissection diagnosed
Patient experienced severe headache and neurological symptoms after swimming

treatments.outcome: Missed diagnosis of carotid artery dissection

allegations.allegation: Failure to diagnose carotid artery dissection

court_findings.finding: Directed verdict for defendant due to lack of expert testimony on causation

court_findings.legal_significance: No breach of standard of care found
